title: Grapes Impact on Cardiovascular Disease. More specific research has been conducted with resveratrol, a well-known compound found in grapes. Resveratrol is recognized for its potent antioxidant properties and also offers anti-inflammatory and heart-protective benefits. However, its development as a drug faces challenges, such as low solubility, poor bioavailability, and possible side effects. There are several clinical trials conducted so far, and proposed strategies are to make resveratrol a viable therapeutic option. Resveratrol has demonstrated significant protective effects in heart cells under ischemia-reperfusion conditions. Studies have found that resveratrol significantly improves heart function in rats and prevents an increase in autophagy caused by ischemia-reperfusion. It may protect against heart injury by modulating the D J-1 and mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase kinase 1 and c-Jun N-terminal Kinase pathways, suggesting a novel approach for maintaining heart health.

sentences.text: Grapes Impact on Cardiovascular Disease.
More specific research has been conducted with resveratrol, a well-known compound found in grapes.
Resveratrol is recognized for its potent antioxidant properties and also offers anti-inflammatory and heart-protective benefits.
However, its development as a drug faces challenges, such as low solubility, poor bioavailability, and possible side effects.
There are several clinical trials conducted so far, and proposed strategies are to make resveratrol a viable therapeutic option.
Resveratrol has demonstrated significant protective effects in heart cells under ischemia-reperfusion conditions.
Studies have found that resveratrol significantly improves heart function in rats and prevents an increase in autophagy caused by ischemia-reperfusion.

It may protect against heart injury by modulating the D J-1 and mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase kinase 1 and c-Jun N-terminal Kinase pathways, suggesting a novel approach for maintaining heart health.

Tome-Carneiro and colleagues investigated the effects of resveratrol-rich grape supplements on the inflammatory and fibrinolytic status of subjects at high risk of cardiovascular disease and treated with statins.

Seventy-five patients consumed either a placebo, a resveratrol-rich grape supplement containing 8 milligrams resveratrol, or a conventional grape supplement lacking resveratrol for the first 6 months and a double dose for the next 6 months.

In contrast to placebo and conventional grape supplements, the resveratrol-rich grape supplement significantly decreased high sensitivity C-reactive protein, T N F-alpha, plasminogen activator inhibitor type 1, and I L-6 to I L-10 ratio but increased anti-inflammatory I L-10.

Tome-Carneiro and colleagues examined the effect of resveratrol-containing grape extracts on the transcriptional profiles of inflammatory genes in peripheral blood mononuclear cells in patients with stable coronary artery disease.

After 1 year of supplementation, in contrast to the placebo and conventional grape extract groups, the resveratrol-containing grape extract group showed an increase in the anti-inflammatory serum adiponectin.
In addition, six key inflammation-related transcription factors were predicted to be significantly activated or inhibited.
Twenty-seven extracellular-space-acting genes involved in inflammation, cell migration, and T-cell interaction signals were downregulated by grape extract.

These authors also investigated the molecular changes in peripheral blood mononuclear cells associated with a 1-year intake of a resveratrol-enriched grape extract in hypertensive male patients with type 2 diabetes.
Supplementation reduced serum I L-6.

The peripheral blood mononuclear cell expression of the pro-inflammatory cytokines C-C motif chemokine ligand 3, I L-1 beta, and T N F-alpha was significantly reduced, and the expression of the transcriptional repressor leucine-rich repeat flightless-interacting protein-1 was increased in peripheral blood mononuclear cells.
Also, many micro R N As involved in the regulation of inflammation, including miR-21, miR-181b, miR-663, miR-30c2, miR-155, and miR-34a, were highly correlated and altered by grape extract.
Clinical Evidence.
Several clinical studies have supported the beneficial effects of grape polyphenolics on cholesterol levels.

For instance, a systematic review and meta-analysis published in the British Journal of Nutrition found that grape seed extract supplementation decreased serum levels of L D L cholesterol and triglycerides and supports the beneficial effects of grape polyphenolics on cholesterol levels.
Although the study did not find a significant overall effect on H D L cholesterol, it did report improvements in studies with shorter intervention periods and lower dosages of grape seed extract.
Another study highlighted the role of resveratrol, a specific polyphenolic found in grape skins, in reducing cholesterol levels and improving heart health.
Incorporating grape polyphenolics into the diet can be achieved through the consumption of grapes, grape juice, and moderate amounts of red wine.
Additionally, grape seed extract supplements are available for those seeking concentrated doses of these beneficial compounds.
Grape polyphenolics offer a natural and effective approach to managing hypercholesterolemia and improving cholesterol levels.
Their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, along with their ability to prevent lipid damage, make them a valuable addition to a heart-healthy lifestyle.
Clinical studies have shown that grape polyphenolics have been found to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation, which are key factors in the development of metabolic syndrome.
By incorporating grape polyphenolics into the diet, individuals can take proactive steps toward better metabolic health and a reduced risk of type 2 diabetes or heart disease.
